objects.name: CyberNetSec.io
objects.description: CyberNetSec.io is a cybersecurity intelligence platform providing timely, accurate, deduplicated, and actionable threat intelligence for security professionals. The platform aggregates, deduplicates, and enriches threat data from diverse public sources, mapping all threats to MITRE ATT&CK and D3FEND frameworks with operationally-focused analysis, detection guidance, and mitigation recommendations. Operated by security professionals, for the security community.

objects.name: Jason Gomes
objects.description: Cybersecurity practitioner and founder of CyberNetSec.io with over 10 years of specialized experience in security operations, threat intelligence, incident response, and security automation. Expertise spans SOAR/XSOAR orchestration, threat intelligence platforms, SIEM/UEBA analytics, and cyber fusion centers.

objects.name: Evil Corp
objects.description: A Russian cybercrime group identified as the operator of the SocGholish malware delivery network. The group functions as a major initial access broker, facilitating ransomware and espionage campaigns by selling access gained through SocGholish.

objects.name: Drive-by Compromise
objects.description: SocGholish operators compromised legitimate websites (WordPress, Joomla, Drupal) to inject malicious JavaScript, which then infected visitors via a drive-by compromise model.

objects.name: User Execution
objects.description: The attack relies on social engineering, tricking the user into downloading and executing a malicious file disguised as a browser update.

objects.name: Malicious File
objects.description: Users are lured by a fake browser update prompt to download and execute a malicious ZIP archive containing a JScript loader.

objects.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: JavaScript
objects.description: Malicious JavaScript was injected into legitimate websites to profile visitors and serve fake update lures. The initial payload was also a JScript loader.

objects.name: Ingress Tool Transfer
objects.description: The initial SocGholish loader, Gholoader, communicates with a C2 server to download and execute second-stage payloads like ransomware and RATs.

objects.name: SocGholish
objects.description: A JavaScript-based malware loader distributed via drive-by compromise on legitimate websites using fake browser update lures. It serves as an initial access broker, delivering various second-stage payloads.

objects.name: LockBit
objects.description: A ransomware family delivered as a second-stage payload by the SocGholish malware.

objects.name: RansomHub
objects.description: A ransomware family delivered as a second-stage payload by the SocGholish malware.

objects.name: Dridex
objects.description: A banking trojan delivered as a second-stage payload by the SocGholish malware.

objects.name: Raspberry Robin
objects.description: A malware loader delivered as a second-stage payload by the SocGholish malware.

objects.name: AsyncRAT
objects.description: A remote access trojan delivered as a second-stage payload by the SocGholish malware.

objects.name: NetSupport RAT
objects.description: A legitimate remote access tool delivered as a second-stage payload by SocGholish to enable remote control of infected systems.

objects.name: Operation Endgame
objects.description: An international law enforcement operation that successfully disrupted the infrastructure of the SocGholish malware network, involving the takedown of C2 servers and the cleaning of thousands of compromised websites.

objects.name: SocGholish C2 Infrastructure
objects.description: A network of 106 command-and-control servers that formed the backbone of the SocGholish botnet. This infrastructure was seized or sinkholed during Operation Endgame.

objects.name: SocGholish Distribution Network
objects.description: A network of approximately 15,000 compromised websites (including WordPress, Joomla, and Drupal sites) used to host malicious JavaScript and fake update lures to distribute the SocGholish malware.

objects.name: SocGholish Fake Update ZIP Download
objects.description: Detects the download of ZIP archives commonly named as fake browser updates, a known TTP of the SocGholish malware.

objects.name: SocGholish Fake Update ZIP Download
objects.description: Detects the download of ZIP archives commonly named as fake browser updates, a known TTP of the SocGholish malware.
objects.pattern:
title: SocGholish Fake Update ZIP Download
id: 2b9f3c1a-4d7e-4b8a-9c1d-8e7f6a5b4c3d
status: experimental
description: Detects the download of ZIP archives commonly named as fake browser updates, a TTP of the SocGholish malware.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/15000-wordpress-websites-cleaned-up-in-socgholish-botnet-takedown/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-20
tags:
  - attack.t1204.002
  - attack.t1189
level: high
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    c-uri|endswith:
      - '/Chrome_update.zip'
  condition: selection

objects.name: SocGholish JScript Loader Execution
objects.description: Detects the execution of a JScript file via wscript.exe, a common execution method for the SocGholish loader after a user is tricked into opening the malicious ZIP file.

objects.name: SocGholish JScript Loader Execution
objects.description: Detects the execution of a JScript file via wscript.exe, a common execution method for the SocGholish loader after a user is tricked into opening the malicious ZIP file.
objects.pattern:
title: SocGholish JScript Loader Execution
id: 320a8f8f-a7b2-44fb-8e82-4dcc1f6a0a4a
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of a JScript file via wscript.exe, a common execution method for the SocGholish loader.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/15000-wordpress-websites-cleaned-up-in-socgholish-botnet-takedown/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-20
tags:
  - attack.t1059.007
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\wscript.exe'
    CommandLine|contains:
      - '.js'
      - '.jse'
  condition: selection

objects.name: Operation Endgame: Global Law Enforcement Disrupts SocGholish, Cleans 15,000 Infected Websites
objects.description:
## Executive Summary

An international law enforcement effort, part of the broader **Operation Endgame**, has successfully disrupted the infrastructure of the **[SocGholish](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0607/)** malware network. The operation, a collaboration between authorities in the U.S., Germany, the Netherlands, and Canada with support from **Europol**, resulted in the takedown of 106 command-and-control (C&C) servers. Additionally, the coalition cleaned malware from 14,971 compromised **[WordPress](https://wordpress.org/)** websites that were being used to distribute the malware. SocGholish is a major initial access broker (IAB) linked to the Russian cybercrime group **[Evil Corp](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0114/)** (also known as Indrik Spider). The disruption strikes a significant blow against a key facilitator of ransomware and other high-impact cyberattacks.

## Incident Timeline

-   **2017:** SocGholish malware first observed in the wild.
-   **Ongoing:** The group compromises thousands of websites, primarily running WordPress, and uses them as a distribution platform.
-   **June 18-19, 2026:** As part of Operation Endgame, law enforcement agencies seize 106 C&C servers and domains used by SocGholish.
-   **June 19, 2026:** Authorities announce the successful disruption and the cleaning of nearly 15,000 infected websites.

## Response Actions

The coordinated operation involved several key actions:
1.  **Infrastructure Takedown:** Law enforcement agencies in multiple countries worked together to seize or sinkhole 106 servers that formed the backbone of the SocGholish C&C network. This action severed the connection between the infected websites and the attackers, preventing them from issuing new commands or deploying further payloads.
2.  **Victim Notification and Remediation:** The operation identified 14,971 compromised WordPress sites. Law enforcement, in conjunction with cybersecurity partners, removed the malicious JavaScript and backdoors from these sites. The owners of the affected websites were notified and provided with guidance to secure their platforms, including changing credentials and applying updates.
3.  **Intelligence Gathering:** The seized infrastructure will be analyzed to gather intelligence on the operators, identify more victims, and understand the full scope of the operation.

## Technical Findings

**SocGholish**, also known as **FakeUpdates**, operates using a drive-by compromise model (**[T1189 - Drive-by Compromise](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1189/)**). The attack flow is as follows:

1.  **Website Compromise:** The attackers exploit vulnerabilities in content management systems (CMS) like WordPress, Joomla, and Drupal to inject malicious JavaScript into legitimate websites.
2.  **Visitor Profiling:** When a user visits an infected site, the malicious script executes in their browser. It profiles the visitor's system to determine if it is a suitable target (e.g., not a security researcher, within a specific geographic region).
3.  **Social Engineering:** If the visitor is deemed a target, the script displays a convincing but fake browser update prompt (e.g., for Chrome or Firefox). This lure tricks the user into downloading and executing a malicious file, which is often a ZIP archive containing a JScript loader.
4.  **Payload Delivery:** The initial loader, often referred to as **Gholoader**, establishes a foothold and communicates with the C&C server. It then downloads and executes second-stage payloads. SocGholish is known to be a delivery mechanism for a wide variety of malware, including:
    *   Ransomware: **[LockBit](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0613/)**, RansomHub
    *   Banking Trojans: **[Dridex](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0384/)**
    *   Loaders and RATs: **Raspberry Robin**, **AsyncRAT**, **NetSupport RAT**

This makes SocGholish a critical link in the cybercrime supply chain, providing the initial access needed for some of the world's most damaging ransomware attacks.

## Lessons Learned

-   **Effectiveness of International Cooperation:** This operation highlights the success of coordinated, public-private partnerships in combating global cybercrime infrastructure.
-   **The Importance of IABs:** The focus on disrupting an Initial Access Broker like SocGholish is a strategic move that has a cascading effect, disrupting the operations of numerous other cybercrime groups that rely on them.
-   **CMS Security is Critical:** The large number of compromised WordPress sites underscores the ongoing challenge of securing popular web platforms. Unpatched plugins, weak credentials, and lack of monitoring remain common entry points.

## Mitigation Recommendations

For website owners:
-   **Patch Management:** Keep your CMS (WordPress, Joomla, etc.) and all plugins/themes updated to the latest versions. This is a form of **[M1051 - Update Software](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1051/)**.
-   **Credential Security:** Use strong, unique passwords for all administrative accounts and enable **[Multi-factor Authentication (M1032)](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1032/)**.
-   **File Integrity Monitoring:** Use security plugins or services to monitor for unauthorized changes to your website's files.

For end-users and organizations:
-   **User Training:** Train users to be suspicious of unsolicited browser update prompts and to only download updates from official sources. This aligns with **[M1017 - User Training](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1017/)**.
-   **Ad Blockers/Script Blockers:** Use web filtering or script-blocking browser extensions to prevent malicious JavaScript from executing.
-   **Execution Prevention:** Configure systems to block the execution of JScript files (`.js`, `.jse`) by default.
